solution:
The claim holds with $c=-\frac{1}{2}$.
Set $t := 1/(1-x)$, so that $x = 1 - 1/t$
and
\[
- \frac{1}{t} - \frac{1}{t^2} \leq \log x \leq - \frac{1}{t}.
\]
Set also $m := \lfloor t \rfloor$.
In the following arguments, we use $c$ to refer to some positive constant independent of $n$ and $t$,
but a different such constant at each appearance.

Suppose first that $a > -\frac{1}{2}$. Then
\begin{align*}
F_a(x)e^{-t} &= \sum_{n=1}^\infty n^a e^{2n-t} x^{n^2}  \\
&\geq \sum_{n=1}^\infty n^a e^{2n-t-n^2/t-n^2/t^2}  \\
&= \sum_{n=1}^\infty n^a e^{-n^2/t^2} e^{-t(1-n/t)^2}.
\end{align*}
If we restrict the sum to the range $t < n < t + \sqrt{t}$, we may bound the summand from below by
$c t^a$; we then have
$F_a(x) e^{-t} > ct^{a+1/2}$ and this tends to $\infty$ as $t \to \infty$.

Suppose next that $a < -\frac{1}{2}$. Then
\begin{align*}
F_a(x)e^{-t} &= \sum_{n=1}^\infty n^a e^{2n-t} x^{n^2} \\
&\leq \sum_{n=1}^\infty n^a e^{-t(1-n/t)^2}.
\end{align*}
Fix $\epsilon>0$ such that $a+\epsilon < -\frac{1}{2}$.
For the summands with $t - t^{1/2+\epsilon} < n < t + t^{1/2+\epsilon}$, we may bound the summand from above by $ct^a$; this range of the sum is then dominated by
$ct^{a+1/2+\epsilon}$. 
For the summands with $n < t - t^{1/2+\epsilon}$, we may bound the summand by
$n^a e^{-t^{2\epsilon}}$; this range of the sum is then dominated by $t e^{-t^{2\epsilon}}$.
For the summands with $n > t - t^{1/2+\epsilon}$, we may again bound the summand by
$n^a e^{-t^{2\epsilon}}$; this range of the sum is then dominated by $c t^{a+1} e^{-t^{2\epsilon}}$.
Since all three bounds tends to 0 as $t \to \infty$, so then does $F_a(x) e^{-t}$.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long.solution:
The claim holds with $genconst=-\frac{1}{2}$.
Set $variablet := 1/(1-variablex)$, so that $variablex = 1 - 1/variablet$
and
\[
- \frac{1}{variablet} - \frac{1}{variablet^2} \leq \log variablex \leq - \frac{1}{variablet}.
\]
Set also $indexmvar := \lfloor variablet \rfloor$.
In the following arguments, we use $genconst$ to refer to some positive constant independent of $indexvar$ and $variablet$,
but a different such constant at each appearance.

Suppose first that $realparam > -\frac{1}{2}$. Then
\begin{align*}
seriesfunc(variablex)e^{-variablet} &= \sum_{indexvar=1}^\infty indexvar^{realparam} e^{2 indexvar-variablet} variablex^{indexvar^2}  \\
&\geq \sum_{indexvar=1}^\infty indexvar^{realparam} e^{2 indexvar-variablet-indexvar^2/variablet-indexvar^2/variablet^2}  \\
&= \sum_{indexvar=1}^\infty indexvar^{realparam} e^{-indexvar^2/variablet^2} e^{-variablet(1-indexvar/variablet)^2}.
\end{align*}
If we restrict the sum to the range $variablet < indexvar < variablet + \sqrt{variablet}$, we may bound the summand from below by
$genconst\, variablet^{realparam}$; we then have
$seriesfunc(variablex) e^{-variablet} > genconst variablet^{realparam+1/2}$ and this tends to $\infty$ as $variablet \to \infty$.

Suppose next that $realparam < -\frac{1}{2}$. Then
\begin{align*}
seriesfunc(variablex)e^{-variablet} &= \sum_{indexvar=1}^\infty indexvar^{realparam} e^{2 indexvar-variablet} variablex^{indexvar^2} \\
&\leq \sum_{indexvar=1}^\infty indexvar^{realparam} e^{-variablet(1-indexvar/variablet)^2}.
\end{align*}
Fix $smalleps>0$ such that $realparam+smalleps < -\frac{1}{2}$.
For the summands with $variablet - variablet^{1/2+smalleps} < indexvar < variablet + variablet^{1/2+smalleps}$, we may bound the summand from above by $genconst variablet^{realparam}$; this range of the sum is then dominated by
$genconst variablet^{realparam+1/2+smalleps}$. 
For the summands with $indexvar < variablet - variablet^{1/2+smalleps}$, we may bound the summand by
$indexvar^{realparam} e^{-variablet^{2 smalleps}}$; this range of the sum is then dominated by $variablet e^{-variablet^{2 smalleps}}$.
For the summands with $indexvar > variablet - variablet^{1/2+smalleps}$, we may again bound the summand by
$indexvar^{realparam} e^{-variablet^{2 smalleps}}$; this range of the sum is then dominated by $genconst variablet^{realparam+1} e^{-variablet^{2 smalleps}}$.
Since all three bounds tends to 0 as $variablet \to \infty$, so then does $seriesfunc(variablex) e^{-variablet}$.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The claim holds with $driftwood=-\frac{1}{2}$.
Set $pinecrown := 1/(1-riverbank)$, so that $riverbank = 1 - 1/pinecrown$
and
\[
- \frac{1}{pinecrown} - \frac{1}{pinecrown^{2}} \leq \log riverbank \leq - \frac{1}{pinecrown}.
\]
Set also $foxglove := \lfloor pinecrown \rfloor$.
In the following arguments, we use $driftwood$ to refer to some positive constant independent of $moonstone$ and $pinecrown$,
but a different such constant at each appearance.

Suppose first that $shoreline > -\frac{1}{2}$. Then
\begin{align*}
glowquartz(riverbank)e^{-pinecrown} &= \sum_{moonstone=1}^{\infty} moonstone^{shoreline} e^{2 moonstone-pinecrown} riverbank^{moonstone^{2}}  \\
&\geq \sum_{moonstone=1}^{\infty} moonstone^{shoreline} e^{2 moonstone-pinecrown-moonstone^{2}/pinecrown-moonstone^{2}/pinecrown^{2}}  \\
&= \sum_{moonstone=1}^{\infty} moonstone^{shoreline} e^{-moonstone^{2}/pinecrown^{2}} e^{-pinecrown(1-moonstone/pinecrown)^{2}}.
\end{align*}
If we restrict the sum to the range $pinecrown < moonstone < pinecrown + \sqrt{pinecrown}$, we may bound the summand from below by
$driftwood\, pinecrown^{shoreline}$; we then have
$glowquartz(riverbank) e^{-pinecrown} > driftwood\, pinecrown^{shoreline+1/2}$ and this tends to $\infty$ as $pinecrown \to \infty$.

Suppose next that $shoreline < -\frac{1}{2}$. Then
\begin{align*}
glowquartz(riverbank)e^{-pinecrown} &= \sum_{moonstone=1}^{\infty} moonstone^{shoreline} e^{2 moonstone-pinecrown} riverbank^{moonstone^{2}} \\
&\leq \sum_{moonstone=1}^{\infty} moonstone^{shoreline} e^{-pinecrown(1-moonstone/pinecrown)^{2}}.
\end{align*}
Fix $buttercup>0$ such that $shoreline+buttercup < -\frac{1}{2}$.
For the summands with $pinecrown - pinecrown^{1/2+buttercup} < moonstone < pinecrown + pinecrown^{1/2+buttercup}$, we may bound the summand from above by $driftwood\, pinecrown^{shoreline}$; this range of the sum is then dominated by
$driftwood\, pinecrown^{shoreline+1/2+buttercup}$. 
For the summands with $moonstone < pinecrown - pinecrown^{1/2+buttercup}$, we may bound the summand by
$moonstone^{shoreline} e^{-pinecrown^{2 buttercup}}$; this range of the sum is then dominated by $pinecrown e^{-pinecrown^{2 buttercup}}$.
For the summands with $moonstone > pinecrown - pinecrown^{1/2+buttercup}$, we may again bound the summand by
$moonstone^{shoreline} e^{-pinecrown^{2 buttercup}}$; this range of the sum is then dominated by $driftwood\, pinecrown^{shoreline+1} e^{-pinecrown^{2 buttercup}}$.
Since all three bounds tends to 0 as $pinecrown \to \infty$, so then does $glowquartz(riverbank) e^{-pinecrown}$.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The claim holds with $variable=-\frac{1}{2}$.
Set $timeless := 1/(1-constant)$, so that $constant = 1 - 1/timeless$
and
\[
- \frac{1}{timeless} - \frac{1}{timeless^2} \leq \log constant \leq - \frac{1}{timeless}.
\]
Set also $limitless := \lfloor timeless \rfloor$.
In the following arguments, we use $variable$ to refer to some positive constant independent of $continuum$ and $timeless$,
but a different such constant at each appearance.

Suppose first that $basement > -\frac{1}{2}$. Then
\begin{align*}
emptiness(constant)e^{-timeless} &= \sum_{continuum=1}^\infty continuum^{basement} e^{2continuum-timeless} constant^{continuum^2}  \\
&\geq \sum_{continuum=1}^\infty continuum^{basement} e^{2continuum-timeless-continuum^2/timeless-continuum^2/timeless^2}  \\
&= \sum_{continuum=1}^\infty continuum^{basement} e^{-continuum^2/timeless^2} e^{-timeless(1-continuum/timeless)^2}.
\end{align*}
If we restrict the sum to the range $timeless < continuum < timeless + \sqrt{timeless}$, we may bound the summand from below by
$variable timeless^{basement}$; we then have
$emptiness(constant) e^{-timeless} > variabletimeless^{basement+1/2}$ and this tends to $\infty$ as $timeless \to \infty$.

Suppose next that $basement < -\frac{1}{2}$. Then
\begin{align*}
emptiness(constant)e^{-timeless} &= \sum_{continuum=1}^\infty continuum^{basement} e^{2continuum-timeless} constant^{continuum^2} \\
&\leq \sum_{continuum=1}^\infty continuum^{basement} e^{-timeless(1-continuum/timeless)^2}.
\end{align*}
Fix $magnitude>0$ such that $basement+magnitude < -\frac{1}{2}$.
For the summands with $timeless - timeless^{1/2+magnitude} < continuum < timeless + timeless^{1/2+magnitude}$, we may bound the summand from above by $variabletimeless^{basement}$; this range of the sum is then dominated by
$variabletimeless^{basement+1/2+magnitude}$. 
For the summands with $continuum < timeless - timeless^{1/2+magnitude}$, we may bound the summand by
$continuum^{basement} e^{-timeless^{2magnitude}}$; this range of the sum is then dominated by $timeless e^{-timeless^{2magnitude}}$.
For the summands with $continuum > timeless - timeless^{1/2+magnitude}$, we may again bound the summand by
$continuum^{basement} e^{-timeless^{2magnitude}}$; this range of the sum is then dominated by $variable timeless^{basement+1} e^{-timeless^{2magnitude}}$.
Since all three bounds tends to 0 as $timeless \to \infty$, so then does $emptiness(constant) e^{-timeless}$.

garbled_string.solution:
The claim holds with $wlenrtuv=-\frac{1}{2}$.
Set $pldmqner := 1/(1-hjgrksla)$, so that $hjgrksla = 1 - 1/pldmqner$
and
\[
- \frac{1}{pldmqner} - \frac{1}{pldmqner^2} \leq \log hjgrksla \leq - \frac{1}{pldmqner}.
\]
Set also $vbschfou := \lfloor pldmqner \rfloor$.
In the following arguments, we use $wlenrtuv$ to refer to some positive constant independent of $qzxwvtnp$ and $pldmqner$,
but a different such constant at each appearance.

Suppose first that $ksdjfpow > -\frac{1}{2}$. Then
\begin{align*}
fhgqupaz(hjgrksla)e^{-pldmqner} &= \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^\infty qzxwvtnp^{ksdjfpow} e^{2qzxwvtnp-pldmqner} hjgrksla^{qzxwvtnp^2}  \\
&\geq \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^\infty qzxwvtnp^{ksdjfpow} e^{2qzxwvtnp-pldmqner-qzxwvtnp^2/pldmqner-qzxwvtnp^2/pldmqner^2}  \\
&= \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^\infty qzxwvtnp^{ksdjfpow} e^{-qzxwvtnp^2/pldmqner^2} e^{-pldmqner(1-qzxwvtnp/pldmqner)^2}.
\end{align*}
If we restrict the sum to the range $pldmqner < qzxwvtnp < pldmqner + \sqrt{pldmqner}$, we may bound the summand from below by
$wlenrtuv pldmqner^{ksdjfpow}$; we then have
$fhgqupaz(hjgrksla) e^{-pldmqner} > wlenrtuv pldmqner^{ksdjfpow+1/2}$ and this tends to $\infty$ as $pldmqner \to \infty$.

Suppose next that $ksdjfpow < -\frac{1}{2}$. Then
\begin{align*}
fhgqupaz(hjgrksla)e^{-pldmqner} &= \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^\infty qzxwvtnp^{ksdjfpow} e^{2qzxwvtnp-pldmqner} hjgrksla^{qzxwvtnp^2} \\
&\leq \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^\infty qzxwvtnp^{ksdjfpow} e^{-pldmqner(1-qzxwvtnp/pldmqner)^2}.
\end{align*}
Fix $gmxvckpu>0$ such that $ksdjfpow+gmxvckpu < -\frac{1}{2}$.
For the summands with $pldmqner - pldmqner^{1/2+gmxvckpu} < qzxwvtnp < pldmqner + pldmqner^{1/2+gmxvckpu}$, we may bound the summand from above by $wlenrtuv pldmqner^{ksdjfpow}$; this range of the sum is then dominated by
$wlenrtuv pldmqner^{ksdjfpow+1/2+gmxvckpu}$. 
For the summands with $qzxwvtnp < pldmqner - pldmqner^{1/2+gmxvckpu}$, we may bound the summand by
$qzxwvtnp^{ksdjfpow} e^{-pldmqner^{2gmxvckpu}}$; this range of the sum is then dominated by $pldmqner e^{-pldmqner^{2gmxvckpu}}$.
For the summands with $qzxwvtnp > pldmqner - pldmqner^{1/2+gmxvckpu}$, we may again bound the summand by
$qzxwvtnp^{ksdjfpow} e^{-pldmqner^{2gmxvckpu}}$; this range of the sum is then dominated by $wlenrtuv pldmqner^{ksdjfpow+1} e^{-pldmqner^{2gmxvckpu}}$.
Since all three bounds tends to 0 as $pldmqner \to \infty$, so then does $fhgqupaz(hjgrksla) e^{-pldmqner}$.

kernel_variant.question:
For a real number b define

g_b(x)=\sum_{n\ge 1} n^{\,b}\,e^{2n}\,x^{n^{2}},\qquad 0\le x<1.

Find a real number d such that

\[
\lim_{x\to 1^-} g_b(x)\,\exp\!\Bigl(-\tfrac{1}{1-x}\Bigr)=0 \quad\text{for all } b<d,\qquad
\lim_{x\to 1^-} g_b(x)\,\exp\!\Bigl(-\tfrac{1}{1-x}\Bigr)=\infty \quad\text{for all } b>d.
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Answer: d = -\tfrac12.

Proof.

1.  Preparations
   Let t := 1/(1-x), so that x = 1-1/t and t \to \infty as x \to 1^-.
   We will frequently use the bounds
   \[
      -\frac1t-\frac1{t^{2}} \;\le\; \log x \;\le\; -\frac1t.\tag{1}
   \]
   With this change of variables
   \[
        g_b(x)\,e^{-t}
      = \sum_{n\ge 1} n^{b}\,e^{2n-t}\,x^{n^{2}}
      = \sum_{n\ge 1} n^{b}\exp\bigl(2n-t+n^{2}\log x\bigr).\tag{2}
   \]

2.  Lower bound when b > -1/2

   We employ the lower estimate in (1): \log x \ge -1/t-1/t^{2}.  Then
   \[
      2n-t+n^{2}\log x \ge 2n-t-\frac{n^{2}}t-\frac{n^{2}}{t^{2}}
                       = -t\Bigl(1-\frac{n}{t}\Bigr)^{2}-\frac{n^{2}}{t^{2}}.\tag{3}
   \]
   Restrict the sum in (2) to the window
   \[
           t < n < t+\sqrt{t}.\tag{4}
   \]
   For such n we have |1-n/t| \le 1/\sqrt{t}, hence by (3)
   2n-t+n^{2}\log x \ge -1-\mathcal{O}(1/t). Thus every term in (2)
   belonging to the window (4) satisfies
   \[
       n^{b}\exp(2n-t+n^{2}\log x) \ge c\,n^{b}\ge c'\,t^{b},\tag{5}
   \]
   for suitable positive constants c,c'.  The number of indices n in (4)
   is \asymp\sqrt{t}, so (2) yields
   \[
      g_b(x)\,e^{-t}\;\ge\;C\,t^{b+1/2},\tag{6}
   \]
   where C>0 is independent of t.  Since b+1/2>0, the right-hand side
   tends to +\infty.  Therefore
   \[
       \lim_{x\to1^-} g_b(x)\,e^{-t}=\infty \qquad (b>-\tfrac12).\tag{7}
   \]

3.  Upper bound when b < -1/2

   Now use the upper estimate in (1): \log x \le -1/t.  From (2)
   2n-t+n^{2}\log x \le 2n-t-n^{2}/t = -t(1-n/t)^{2}.  Consequently
   \[
       g_b(x)\,e^{-t}\;\le\;\sum_{n\ge1} n^{b}\,e^{-t(1-n/t)^{2}}.\tag{8}
   \]
   Fix \varepsilon>0 with b+\varepsilon<-1/2.

   *  Central region: |n-t| \le t^{1/2+\varepsilon}.  Here
      e^{-t(1-n/t)^{2}}\le 1, so the contribution is \(\ll
      t^{b+1/2+\varepsilon}\to0\).

   *  Left tail: n < t-t^{1/2+\varepsilon}.  Then
      (1-n/t)^{2}\ge t^{2\varepsilon}/t = t^{2\varepsilon-1}, whence
      e^{-t(1-n/t)^{2}}\le e^{-t^{2\varepsilon}}.  The whole tail sums to
      \(\ll t\,e^{-t^{2\varepsilon}}\to0\).

   *  Right tail: n > t+t^{1/2+\varepsilon}.  The treatment is identical.

   Putting the three pieces together gives
   \[
      g_b(x)\,e^{-t}\;\longrightarrow\;0 \qquad (b<-\tfrac12).\tag{9}
   \]

4.  Threshold value
   Combining (7) and (9) we see that
   \[
      \lim_{x\to1^-} g_b(x)\,e^{-t}=
      \begin{cases}
         0 & (b< -\tfrac12),\\
         \infty & (b> -\tfrac12).
      \end{cases}
   \]
   Hence the required separating constant is d= -1/2.

5.  Remark on the critical index b = -1/2
   The problem does not ask for the limit when b=-1/2; with the standard
   saddle-point estimates one finds that g_{-1/2}(x)\,e^{-t} stays
   bounded away from both 0 and \infty.

Thus d=-1/2 is the unique threshold.

core_steps: Re-parametrize with t = 1/(1-x) so that x→1⁻ corresponds to t→∞.
Use elementary bounds on log(1−1/t) to approximate x^{n²} and obtain an exponent of the form −n²/t − t(1−n/t)² (plus harmless O(n²/t²)).
Factor out e^{-t} and recognize a Gaussian weight e^{-t(1−n/t)²}, locating the main contribution at n≈t with natural width √t.
Estimate the sum by splitting n into a ‘central’ band (|n−t|≲√t) and the two ‘tail’ regions; compare the total size with t^{a+1/2}.
Deduce that the sum grows like t^{a+1/2}, giving divergence for a>−1/2 and vanishing for a<−1/2, hence c = −1/2.

slot1.description: The exact constant multiplying 1/t² in the lower bound for log x; any positive constant would suffice for the error term.

slot2.description: The precise width chosen for the ‘central’ band around n≈t; any band of order √t (e.g. t < n < t+k√t for fixed k>0) works.

slot3.description: The small positive ε used to thicken the band in the upper estimate; only ε>0 with a+ε<−1/2 is needed.

slot4.description: The undetermined positive constant c repeatedly used in inequalities; its actual value is irrelevant.
